tools.name: whoami
tools.description: Verifies your redu.cloud credential with a REAL server round-trip and returns your identity + live quota. A green result means the key actually works — not merely that one is configured. Use this first if anything is 401ing.

tools.name: check_deploy_prerequisites
tools.description: The deploy ENTRY GATE — run this first when a user wants to deploy. Verifies the account is ready and AUTO-SELECTS the network_id + keypair_name to pass to deploy_app (so you never hunt for them): (1) account reachable + quota, (2) a private network (picks your default — every account has one), (3)

tools.name: deploy_overview
tools.description: Orientation for deploying an app on redu.cloud: the end-to-end flow, the two source modes (git vs upload), how to handle a missing Dockerfile or a database, when (and when NOT) to split a deploy across multiple VMs, verifying a deploy functionally, and how to debug a failed build. Call this first wh

tools.name: create_api_key
tools.description: Mints a NEW least-privilege redu API key for a deployed app or automation to use (e.g. to add a backup feature). GUARDRAIL: the first call does NOT create anything. It returns a confirmation the user must approve, because a key is a long-lived credential that acts on the account and ANY usage of it

tools.name: integrate_overview
tools.description: Orientation for wiring a redu.cloud capability (backups, DNS, extra storage, a managed DB, ...) INTO an app already deployed on redu, e.g. 'add a backup feature to the Supabase I deployed on redu'. Explains the pattern: mint a LEAST-PRIVILEGE scoped API key (with the user's approval via create_api_k

tools.name: prepare_upload
tools.description: Returns the LOCAL shell commands to package your working directory and upload it for an upload-mode deploy (no git, no PAT). Run them in the user's terminal, capture `source_token` from the upload's JSON response, then call deploy_app with that source_token (omit repo). The upload authenticates AUTO

tools.name: scaffold_local
tools.description: OPTIONAL preflight: returns a podman-compose.yml + .env so the user can run the app (and a throwaway local Postgres) on THEIR machine before deploying to redu — to see it run / sanity-check the container. Requires local podman/podman-compose. It's a suggestion, not a gate — skip it and go straight t

tools.name: plan_instance
tools.description: Aggregates images/flavors/keypairs/networks/security groups into human-friendly choices. Does not create anything. Happy path: import_keypair → plan_instance → create_instance → get_ssh_command. Call this second (after import_keypair, if you have no keypairs).

tools.name: plan_managed_datastore
tools.description: Plans a direct managed datastore create without provisioning anything. Use this before create_database, create_relational_database, create_redis, or create_clickhouse. For app deployments, prefer deploy_app database:'managed' so plan_deploy includes and wires the datastore automatically.

tools.name: select_surface
tools.description: For a repo with SEVERAL runnable parts, call this BEFORE plan_deploy. You enumerate the candidate surfaces (find every Dockerfile/Containerfile, OPEN each, classify by its EXPOSE + CMD — never by directory name) and pass them in; redu RANKS them with fixed rules (a standalone browser desktop/noVNC >

tools.name: plan_deploy
tools.description: Turns YOUR repo classification (you scan the repo and pass what you found) into a complete, approvable deploy plan WITHOUT creating anything: picks the VM + managed-Postgres sizes, prices them at the real pricing_rules rates, and checks they FIT your quota — so a plan that can't provision is caught

tools.name: list_instances
tools.description: Lists your compute instances.

tools.name: list_flavors
tools.description: Lists available instance sizes.

tools.name: list_images
tools.description: Lists available OS images.

tools.name: list_keypairs
tools.description: Lists your SSH keypairs. If empty, call import_keypair first before creating instances.

tools.name: list_security_groups
tools.description: Lists your security groups.

tools.name: list_private_networks
tools.description: Lists your private networks.

tools.name: list_volumes
tools.description: Lists your block storage volumes.

tools.name: list_snapshots
tools.description: Lists your instance snapshots.

tools.name: list_backups
tools.description: Lists your volume backups.

tools.name: list_clusters
tools.description: Lists your autoscaling clusters.

tools.name: list_dns_entries
tools.description: Lists DNS proxy host entries.

tools.name: list_domains
tools.description: Lists custom domains you have verified ownership of.

tools.name: list_regions
tools.description: Lists available regions.

tools.name: list_databases
tools.description: Lists your managed PostgreSQL databases. Once a row's status is 'ready', it carries the private-network connection details (private_ip, port 5432, db_name, db_user).

tools.name: list_relational_databases
tools.description: Lists your managed MySQL/MariaDB databases (the relational-database resource). Each row carries its engine ('mysql'|'mariadb'); once status is 'ready' it has the private-network connection details (private_ip, port 3306, db_name, db_user).

tools.name: list_clickhouse_databases
tools.description: Lists your managed ClickHouse databases (OLAP / analytics — its own resource, not a relational DB). Once a row's status is 'ready' it carries the private-network connection details (private_ip, ClickHouse HTTP port 8123, db_name, db_user).

tools.name: list_redis
tools.description: Lists your managed Redis instances. Once a row's status is 'ready' it carries the private-network connection details (private_ip, port 6379) — connect from another instance on the same private network with redis-cli -h <private_ip> -p 6379 -a <password>.

tools.name: import_keypair
tools.description: Registers an existing SSH public key on your account. Use this to import your own public key so you can SSH into instances. The private key never leaves your machine.

tools.name: delete_keypair
tools.description: Removes an SSH keypair from your account by name.

tools.name: get_ssh_command
tools.description: Returns the SSH command to connect to an instance via the redu.cloud TCP proxy. For a DEPLOYMENT VM (created by deploy_app/deploy_compose) pass keypair_name — read it from get_deployment — so the command uses `-i ~/.ssh/<keypair_name>` and authenticates with the RIGHT key instead of your default ide

tools.name: get_instance_logs
tools.description: Returns the console log output from an instance.

tools.name: instance_action
tools.description: Start, stop, or reboot an instance. action must be START, STOP, REBOOT_SOFT, or REBOOT_HARD.

tools.name: create_instance
tools.description: Creates a raw compute instance for custom OS/cloud-init workflows. For app/source deployments, prefer check_deploy_prerequisites -> plan_deploy -> deploy_app/deploy_compose; do not use create_instance as a shortcut around the deployment plan. Requires a recent plan_instance. For reduOS, set cloud_in

tools.name: delete_instance
tools.description: Permanently deletes an instance. This cannot be undone.

tools.name: create_database
tools.description: Provisions a managed PostgreSQL database on a dedicated VM on your private network. Requires a recent plan_managed_datastore. For app deployments, prefer deploy_app database:'managed' so plan_deploy includes and wires the DB automatically. It is PRIVATE — reachable only from another instance on the

tools.name: delete_database
tools.description: Deletes a managed Postgres database and its underlying VM. Pass the numeric database id from list_databases. This cannot be undone.

tools.name: create_relational_database
tools.description: Provisions a managed MySQL (or MariaDB) database on a dedicated VM on your private network — the relational-database resource (use this instead of create_database when the app needs MySQL/MariaDB, e.g. WordPress, NextCloud, Matomo, many PHP/LAMP apps). Requires a recent plan_managed_datastore. For a

tools.name: delete_relational_database
tools.description: Deletes a managed MySQL/MariaDB database and its underlying VM. Pass the numeric id from list_relational_databases. This cannot be undone.

tools.name: create_clickhouse
tools.description: Provisions a managed ClickHouse database (OLAP / columnar analytics engine, Apache-2.0) on a dedicated VM on your private network — its OWN resource, NOT a relational database. Requires a recent plan_managed_datastore. Use it for analytics / observability workloads that need a column store (PostHog,

tools.name: delete_clickhouse
tools.description: Deletes a managed ClickHouse database and its underlying VM. Pass the numeric id from list_clickhouse_databases. This cannot be undone.

tools.name: create_redis
tools.description: Provisions a managed Redis instance on a dedicated VM on your private network. Requires a recent plan_managed_datastore. It is PRIVATE — reachable only from another instance on the same private network, via its internal/private IP on port 6379 (not a public address). AUTH (requirepass) is always ena

tools.name: delete_redis
tools.description: Deletes a managed Redis instance and its underlying VM. Pass the numeric id from list_redis. This cannot be undone.

tools.name: deploy_app
tools.description: Deploys an app to a VM and exposes it at a public https://<name>-<id>.redu.cloud URL (a random 8-char suffix is appended to <name> for uniqueness — a BARE custom `dname` like `myapp.redu.cloud` ALSO gets a suffix, so to PIN a known URL pass a dname that already includes an 8-char suffix like `myapp-

tools.name: deploy_compose
tools.description: Deploys a MULTI-CONTAINER app — a repo that ships a docker-compose.yml / compose.yaml (app + its own db/redis/worker containers) — onto ONE VM via podman-compose, and exposes ONE service at https://<name>-<id>.redu.cloud. Use this instead of deploy_app when the repo is a compose stack rather than a

tools.name: deploy_vpn
tools.description: Stands up a redu VPN gateway (WireGuard tunnelled over wstunnel on WSS/443) on your PRIVATE network, so you (or your team) can reach the private IPs of the VMs you deployed on redu, over a single https endpoint — no UDP, no extra ports. This is the TENANT VPN for reaching your OWN deployed resources

tools.name: list_deployments
tools.description: Lists your app deployments. Each row carries status (provisioning/ready/build_failed/error), the current build phase while provisioning (installing/source/building/built/starting), the public access_point URL, port, repo, and build_log on failure.

tools.name: delete_deployment
tools.description: Deletes a deployment and its underlying app VM. Pass the numeric id from list_deployments. IMPORTANT: if the deployment used database:'managed', the managed Postgres VM is NOT deleted (data safety) — this tool returns its id so you can delete_database it when you're done with the data. Cannot be und

tools.name: get_deployment
tools.description: Fetches ONE deployment by its numeric id (from list_deployments). Returns its current status, the public access_point URL, the underlying VM id, AND the build_log — read this when status is 'build_failed' or 'error' to see exactly why the on-VM build/run failed (no SSH needed). Also returns a realit

tools.name: architecture_diagram
tools.description: Read-only. Fetches EVERY resource on your redu.cloud account (VMs, volumes, private networks, managed databases: Postgres/MySQL/MariaDB/ClickHouse/Redis/Qdrant, load balancers, and DNS access points) and renders ONE Mermaid diagram of the whole account, grouped into per-private-network subgraphs wit

tools.name: create_snapshot
tools.description: Creates a snapshot of a running instance.

tools.name: delete_snapshot
tools.description: Deletes a snapshot by ID.

tools.name: create_backup
tools.description: Creates a backup, by volumeId or by instanceId (the instance's volume is resolved for you). The backup is held independently of the instance, so it survives the VM. Poll list_backups until the new backup is available.

tools.name: restore_backup
tools.description: Restores a backup into an existing volume (volumeId), an instance's volume (instanceId), or a new one (volumeName). Poll list_volumes for the restored volume.

tools.name: delete_backup
tools.description: Deletes a backup by ID.

tools.name: create_volume
tools.description: Creates a block storage volume.

tools.name: get_domain_verification
tools.description: Returns the DNS TXT record to add for custom domain ownership verification. Add the record to your DNS provider, then call verify_domain.

tools.name: verify_domain
tools.description: Checks the DNS TXT ownership record for a custom domain and marks it verified when the record is present. Call get_domain_verification first, add the TXT record at your DNS provider, then call this until status is verified.

tools.name: check_agent_prerequisites
tools.description: Checks if your account has everything needed to run autonomous coding agents. Returns a checklist: API key validity, private network, SSH keypair, and billing. Safe to call anytime — read-only. Call this first before create_controller.

tools.name: create_controller
tools.description: Creates a self-configuring controller VM on redu.cloud. Auto-discovers your network, Ubuntu image, and flavor. Generates and uploads an SSH keypair if you don't have one. The VM bootstraps itself — no SSH or VPN needed. After ~7 minutes, authorize the fleet (run the authorize.sh one-liner it returns

tools.name: trigger_agent_batch
tools.description: Starts the autonomous agent batch on your controller VM. Agents read agent-ready tasks from your ROADMAP.md, implement them in parallel, and open PRs. No VPN needed — runs over HTTPS; the controller stays running after you disconnect. Each ROADMAP task MUST be a single checkbox line `- [ ] **Title**

tools.name: get_agent_status
tools.description: Returns the current status of your controller VM. On a freshly set-up VM also returns the trigger token needed to authorize the fleet and to call trigger_agent_batch. Call this after setup_agent_fleet to confirm the VM is ready.

tools.name: setup_agent_session
tools.description: One-shot tool that guides you through the complete agent setup: checks prerequisites, creates the controller VM, and returns next steps. Ideal for first-time setup. Call this when a user says 'set up autocoding agents for my repo'.

tools.name: setup_agent_fleet
tools.description: Complete one-shot setup: validates prerequisites, creates a controller VM + worker VMs, auto-creates a public HTTPS URL on port 7070, seeds a starter ROADMAP.md into the repo if absent, and returns the trigger token. Call this when a user says 'set up autocoding agents for my repo' or 'I want agents

findings.title: Lethal trifecta: untrusted input + private data + external sink
findings.detail: Server "mcp" exposes tools that together enable an exfiltration flow. Untrusted content: deploy_app, deploy_compose, list_deployments, get_deployment, trigger_agent_batch, setup_agent_fleet · Private data: whoami, check_deploy_prerequisites, deploy_overview, create_api_key, integrate_overview, prepare_upload, scaffold_local, plan_managed_datastore, plan_deploy, list_keypairs, list_private_networks, list_databases, list_relational_databases, list_clickhouse_databases, list_redis, import_keypair, delete_keypair, get_ssh_command, create_instance, create_database, delete_database, create_relational_database, delete_relational_database, create_clickhouse, delete_clickhouse, create_redis, deploy_app, deploy_compose, deploy_vpn, delete_deployment, get_deployment, architecture_diagram, get_domain_verification, verify_domain, check_agent_prerequisites, create_controller, get_agent_status, setup_agent_fleet · Public sink: deploy_overview, prepare_upload, deploy_app, deploy_compose · Destructive: check_deploy_prerequisites, prepare_upload, scaffold_local, select_surface, plan_deploy, delete_keypair, delete_instance, delete_database, delete_relational_database, delete_clickhouse, delete_redis, deploy_app, deploy_compose, delete_deployment, get_deployment, delete_snapshot, delete_backup, check_agent_prerequisites, create_controller, trigger_agent_batch, setup_agent_fleet.

evidence.snippet: Untrusted content: deploy_app, deploy_compose, list_deployments, get_deployment, trigger_agent_batch, setup_agent_fleet · Private data: whoami, check_deploy_prerequisites, deploy_overview, create_api_key, integrate_overview, prepare_upload, scaffold_local, plan_managed_datastore, plan_deploy, list_keypairs, list_private_networks, list_databases, list_relational_databases, list_clickhouse_databases, list_redis, import_keypair, delete_keypair, get_ssh_command, create_instance, create_database, delete_database, create_relational_database, delete_relational_database, create_clickhouse, delete_clickhouse, create_redis, deploy_app, deploy_compose, deploy_vpn, delete_deployment, get_deployment, architecture_diagram, get_domain_verification, verify_domain, check_agent_prerequisites, create_controller, get_agent_status, setup_agent_fleet · Public sink: deploy_overview, prepare_upload, deploy_app, deploy_compose · Destructive: check_deploy_prerequisites, prepare_upload, scaffold_local, select_surface, plan_deploy, delete_keypair, delete_instance, delete_database, delete_relational_database, delete_clickhouse, delete_redis, deploy_app, deploy_compose, delete_deployment, get_deployment, delete_snapshot, delete_backup, check_agent_prerequisites, create_controller, trigger_agent_batch, setup_agent_fleet

findings.remediation: Do not co-install these capabilities under one trust boundary; an injected comment/email can steer the model to exfiltrate via the sink tool.
